items.q: Is speed the same thing as velocity?
items.correct: No — speed is a scalar; velocity includes direction, so equal speeds can be different velocities.
items.misconception: Yes — they are interchangeable words for how fast something moves.
items.random_wrong: No — velocity is always larger than speed.
items.why: Everyday language uses the words interchangeably, hiding the vector nature of velocity.

items.q: Can forces of 3 N and 4 N combine to give a resultant of 1 N?
items.correct: Yes — if they act in opposite directions; a vector sum depends on direction.
items.misconception: No — 3 N plus 4 N always makes 7 N.
items.random_wrong: No — forces can never partially cancel.
items.why: Students add magnitudes arithmetically, ignoring that direction determines the resultant of vectors.

items.q: Once you have the number, does the unit still matter?
items.correct: Yes — a value is meaningless without its unit, and units must stay consistent through equations.
items.misconception: No — the number alone is the answer.
items.why: Students drop units in their working, which hides inconsistent equations and order-of-magnitude mistakes.

items.q: Which of these is a vector quantity?
items.why: Correct answer: Force. “Energy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Energy. “Velocity” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Forces of 3 N and 4 N act on an object at right angles. The magnitude of the resultant is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 5 N. “1 N” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A 10 N force acts at 60° above the horizontal. What is its horizontal component?
items.why: The real figure is 5 N. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
